checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the output against the source document. All text present in the output, including headings and paragraphs, corresponds to text found in the source PDF. There are no instances of hallucinated body text or invented section headings.

The source document contains a physical address ('301 North Wilmington Street, 7th Floor'). This address is correctly represented in the output. There are no URLs, email addresses, or phone numbers in the source, and none were fabricated in the output.

While substantial text is missing from Sections 6 and 8, the structural elements (headings and list items representing sub-sections) for all sections and pages are present in the output. Therefore, this is classified as significant text omission within sections (C5) rather than a complete section or page being dropped.
The source document contains no tables; therefore, this check is not applicable.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The output fails to include the descriptive body text for several subsections. Specifically, the entire descriptive content (multiple paragraphs) for Section 6, items a, b, c, d, and e is missing. Additionally, the body text for Section 8, items a and b is missing. These omissions represent approximately 50% of the total text content on pages 2 and 3 of the source document, far exceeding the 25% threshold for a MAJOR defect.
checks.b_evidence_source: The LGC last met on November 20th. All open slots on the committee have been filled... [approx 2 pages of text following subheadings in Section 6 and Section 8]
checks.b_evidence_output: [Content entirely absent after list items in reading_order 24, 26, and 30]
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 6 (Status Reports) and Section 8 (Other Items), Pages 2 and 3

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text that was successfully converted is highly accurate and preserves the original wording and meaning. No problematic differences in numbers, dates, or terminology were found.

checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain any duplicated text blocks. Each paragraph and section header from the source appears only once in the JSON representation, and page headers/footers are correctly excluded or not repeated.

The heading hierarchy is logical and consistent with the source. H1 is used for the committee name at the top, and H2 is used for major sections ('MINUTES', '6. Status Reports', '7. February 12...', etc.). No heading levels are skipped.
The source document contains no tables; therefore, no structural corruption of tables is possible.

The structural elements extracted (headings, paragraphs, lists) appear in the correct sequential order as found in the source document. While significant portions of the body text from sections 6 and 8 are missing (which is a content accuracy issue), the relative reading order of the remaining elements matches the source's intended flow.

The document's list structures are poorly handled. The primary numbered list (1-5) on Page 1 is fragmented into five separate single-item unordered lists (elements 9, 11, 13, 15, 19), and the numbering prefixes are stripped. Furthermore, the alphabetical sub-list in section 6 (a-c) on Page 2 loses its labels ('a.', 'b.', 'c.') and is converted to an unordered list (element 24). In contrast, sub-lists later in the document (elements 26 and 30) retain their labels as part of the text string while still being typed as unordered, showing significant inconsistency in structural representation.
checks.b_evidence_source:
1. Welcome
2. Approval of Minutes...
a. Local Government Committee...
checks.b_evidence_output: The JSON uses "type": "list", "list_type": "unordered" for multiple fragmented items (e.g., elements 9, 11) and strips the prefix 'a.' from the content in element 24.

checks.a_analysis: A hyperlink to a .gov resource is present in the source PDF (section 6.e, on the text 'open source document'). This link is entirely missing from the output because the paragraph containing the link was not captured.
checks.b_evidence_source: An item to work on is an update to the open source document to include...
checks.b_evidence_output: The output contains no link object or text corresponding to the paragraph under section 6.e.

checks.a_analysis: In the source PDF, the meeting minutes are organized into a continuous numbered list from 1 to 9 (1. Welcome, 2. Approval..., through 9. Adjourn), where each number represents a top-level agenda item of equal hierarchy. In the HTML output, the visual organization is significantly altered. Items 1 through 5 are demoted to a bulleted list nested under the 'MINUTES' section, and they are entirely omitted from the navigation Table of Contents. Conversely, items 6, 7, 8, and 9 are rendered as numbered headings at the same hierarchical level as the 'MINUTES' section itself. This creates an inconsistent and incorrect visual hierarchy that does not reflect the source document's structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: Numbered list items 1 through 9 (e.g., '1. Welcome', '2. Approval of Minutes...', up to '9. Adjourn') are top-level headings on pages 1, 2, and 3.
checks.b_evidence_output: Items 1-5 are rendered as a bulleted list within the body and are missing from the TOC (e.g., '* Welcome', '* Approval of Minutes...'), while items 6-9 (e.g., '6. Status Reports') are rendered as primary headings included in the TOC.
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the document structure, specifically comparing the sections before and after item 6.

checks.a_analysis: The source PDF document consists entirely of text and lists; it does not contain any tables. The HTML output correctly reflects this absence.
The source PDF is a text-based document with no logos, charts, photos, or diagrams. The HTML output correctly does not include any image elements.

The body content of the document is rendered once. While headings are repeated in the Table of Contents, this is a standard and expected accessibility feature and does not constitute content duplication.
